tweetText: Anthropic's growing success is making some OpenAI investors reconsider their support. With Anthropic valued at $380 billion, it seems like a better deal compared to OpenAI's lofty $1.2 trillion expectations. via TechCrunch AI

Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark revealed the company briefed the Trump administration on its AI project, Mythos, despite currently suing the government. This highlights the complex relationship between tech firms and regulators in shaping AI policies. via TechCrunch AI
Google is adding AI "Skills" to Chrome, allowing users to save and reuse prompts across websites. This makes it easier to streamline workflows and enhance productivity. via TechCrunch AI

Google has launched its Gemini Personal Intelligence feature in India, allowing users to connect their Google accounts for tailored responses. This enhances user experience by providing more relevant and personalized information. via TechCrunch AI

Google Chrome now features AI-powered 'Skills' in the Gemini sidebar, helping users enhance recipes and summarize YouTube videos. This makes browsing more efficient and fun, streamlining everyday tasks. via WIRED AI

Anthropic is pushing back against an Illinois bill that would protect AI labs from liability in case of major disasters, which OpenAI supports. This clash highlights the ongoing debate over accountability in AI development. via WIRED AI

Silicon Valley is pouring millions into stopping Alex Bores, a former Palantir employee who championed strict AI regulations, from winning a Congressional seat. This highlights the tech industry's fear of stricter oversight on AI. via WIRED AI.

A young man allegedly attacked OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home, fearing AI could lead to human extinction. These incidents highlight rising tensions and fears surrounding AI development. via The Verge AI.
Chrome's new feature lets users save AI prompts as "Skills" for easy reuse across different webpages. This makes it simpler to perform repeated tasks without retyping prompts. via The Verge AI.

A developer claims to have reverse-engineered Google's AI watermarking system, suggesting watermarks can be removed or added easily. This raises concerns about the integrity of AI-generated content. via The Verge AI.
